title: Count elements smaller than X
description: Count the number of elements smaller than a given value using binary search

examples.explanation: Initialize count = 0. Element 3: 3 < 5 → true, count = 1. Element 7: 7 < 5 → false, count = 1. Element 1: 1 < 5 → true, count = 2. Element 9: 9 < 5 → false, count = 2. Element 4: 4 < 5 → true, count = 3. Final count = 3.
Initialize count = 0. Element 10: 10 < 25 → true, count = 1. Element 20: 20 < 25 → true, count = 2. Element 30: 30 < 25 → false, count = 2. Element 40: 40 < 25 → false, count = 2. Final count = 2.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n)
approaches.summary: Iterate through the entire array and count elements less than X by comparing each element directly. This is straightforward and works for unsorted arrays.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Elements Smaller Than X Using Brute Force
ladders.desc: Complete solution to count elements smaller than X by iterating through the entire array
ladders.explanation: The brute force approach solves the problem by examining every element in the array exactly once. The intuition is straightforward: since we need to count elements with values less than X, we can simply check each element against X and increment a counter when the condition is satisfied. This works regardless of whether the array is sorted or not because we're performing a complete linear scan. The algorithm starts by initializing a counter to zero. Then it iterates through each element in the array using a loop. For each element, it compares the current value to X. If the element is strictly less than X, we increment the counter. After processing all elements, the counter contains the final answer. Time complexity is O(n) where n is the array length, as we perform one comparison per element. Space complexity is O(1) since we only use a constant amount of extra space for the counter. This connects to the key subroutine (L1) as it's the complete implementation of that counting logic. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize the counter, using <= instead of < for comparison, or trying to optimize prematurely when the brute force approach is sufficient for the problem constraints. The real code pattern involves a simple for loop with a conditional check.

examples.trace: Initialize count = 0. Element 3: 3 < 5 → true, count = 1. Element 7: 7 < 5 → false, count = 1. Element 1: 1 < 5 → true, count = 2. Element 9: 9 < 5 → false, count = 2. Element 4: 4 < 5 → true, count = 3. Final count = 3.
Initialize count = 0. Element 10: 10 < 25 → true, count = 1. Element 20: 20 < 25 → true, count = 2. Element 30: 30 < 25 → false, count = 2. Element 40: 40 < 25 → false, count = 2. Final count = 2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Linear Scan Counting Function
ladders.desc: Implement a function that counts elements satisfying a condition in an array
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine forms the heart of the brute force solution: a function that takes an array and a value X, then returns the count of elements less than X. The intuition is that counting is a fundamental operation in programming, and linear scanning is the most direct way to examine all elements. The algorithm involves initializing a counter variable to track how many elements meet the condition. We then iterate through the array using an index variable that goes from 0 to length-1. For each position, we access the element and compare it to X. If the comparison returns true (element < X), we increment the counter. After the loop completes, we return the counter value. Time complexity is O(n) for the linear scan. Space complexity is O(1) for the counter variable. This connects to the core logic (L2) as it implements the complete counting mechanism that will be used in the full solution. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in loop boundaries, modifying the array during iteration, or returning inside the loop instead of after completion. The real code pattern is a function with a for loop and conditional increment.

examples.trace: Function called with arr=[1,5,3,7], X=4. Initialize count=0. i=0: arr[0]=1, 1<4 → true, count=1. i=1: arr[1]=5, 5<4 → false, count=1. i=2: arr[2]=3, 3<4 → true, count=2. i=3: arr[3]=7, 7<4 → false, count=2. Return count=2.
Function called with arr=[10,20,30], X=15. Initialize count=0. i=0: arr[0]=10, 10<15 → true, count=1. i=1: arr[1]=20, 20<15 → false, count=1. i=2: arr[2]=30, 30<15 → false, count=1. Return count=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Element Comparison and Conditional Counting
ladders.desc: The process of checking each element against X and conditionally incrementing a counter
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the central mechanism: for each array element, compare it to X and increment a counter only if the element is smaller. The intuition is that counting requires two operations: evaluation (is this element relevant?) and accumulation (keep track of how many relevant elements we've seen). The algorithm works by maintaining a counter variable that starts at 0. As we examine each element in sequence, we perform a comparison: element < X. This comparison yields a boolean result (true or false). If true, we execute count = count + 1. If false, we do nothing and move to the next element. This conditional increment is the core of the counting process. Time complexity for each element is O(1) for the comparison and potential increment. Space complexity is O(1) for the counter. This connects to the building block (L3) as it combines comparison operations with conditional logic to achieve counting. Common mistakes include using = instead of == in comparisons (in languages where that matters), forgetting that comparison should be strict (< not ≤), or trying to optimize the comparison when simple less-than is sufficient. The real code pattern is an if statement inside a loop with a counter variable.

examples.input: Process arr = [2, 8, 3, 6] with X = 5, counter starts at 0
examples.trace: Counter initially 0. Element 2: compare 2 < 5 → true, counter becomes 1. Element 8: compare 8 < 5 → false, counter remains 1. Element 3: compare 3 < 5 → true, counter becomes 2. Element 6: compare 6 < 5 → false, counter remains 2. Final counter = 2.

examples.input: Process arr = [10, 20, 30] with X = 25, counter starts at 0
examples.trace: Counter initially 0. Element 10: compare 10 < 25 → true, counter becomes 1. Element 20: compare 20 < 25 → true, counter becomes 2. Element 30: compare 30 < 25 → false, counter remains 2. Final counter = 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Traversal with Indexing
ladders.desc: Iterating through all elements of an array using index-based access
ladders.explanation: This level addresses how to systematically access each element in an array, which is necessary before we can compare them to X. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations with indices, so we can use a loop variable to access each position sequentially. The algorithm involves determining the array length n, then using a loop variable i that takes values from 0 to n-1 (inclusive). For each value of i, we access the element at arr[i]. This gives us each element exactly once in order. After accessing arr[i], we would typically perform some operation on it (which at higher levels will be comparison with X). Time complexity is O(n) to visit n elements. Space complexity is O(1) for the loop variable. This connects to the basic operation (L4) as it relies on array indexing to retrieve elements. Common mistakes include starting the loop at 1 instead of 0, going up to n instead of n-1 (causing index out of bounds), or modifying the loop variable incorrectly inside the loop body. The real code pattern is a for loop with range(0, len(arr)) or equivalent.

examples.trace: Array length = 3. i=0: access arr[0] = 10. i=1: access arr[1] = 20. i=2: access arr[2] = 30. Loop completes after i=2.
Array length = 4. i=0: access arr[0] = 5. i=1: access arr[1] = 1. i=2: access arr[2] = 9. i=3: access arr[3] = 2. Loop completes after i=3.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access Using Index
ladders.desc: Retrieving the value stored at a specific position in an array
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves accessing an array element given its index. The intuition is that arrays provide random access: given an index i, we can directly retrieve the element at that position in constant time. The algorithm is simple: if we have an array arr and an integer index i (where 0 ≤ i < length of arr), we use the syntax arr[i] to get the value. This operation assumes the index is valid (within bounds). The value returned is whatever is stored at that memory location. Time complexity is O(1) for direct access. Space complexity is O(1) as no additional memory is needed. This connects to the concept foundation (L5) as it builds on understanding what arrays are and how they're indexed. Common mistakes include using 1-based indexing (assuming first element is at index 1), accessing with negative indices (unless the language supports it), or not checking bounds before access. The real code pattern is simply arr[i] where i is a valid integer index.

examples.trace: Array has elements at indices 0,1,2. Accessing index 1: arr[1] returns the value 20.
Array has elements at indices 0,1,2,3. Accessing index 0: arr[0] returns the value 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Basic introduction to arrays as indexed collections of elements
ladders.explanation: This foundational level explains what an array is and how indexing works, which is essential for all higher levels. An array is a data structure that stores a collection of elements, each identified by an index. The intuition is like a row of boxes numbered starting from 0: each box holds a value, and we can refer to any box by its number. Arrays have a fixed size (length) determined when created. Elements are stored contiguously in memory. Indexing starts at 0 in most programming languages, meaning the first element is at position 0, the second at position 1, etc. The last element is at position (length-1). We can store any type of value in arrays (integers, strings, etc.), though for this problem we use integers. Arrays allow us to group related data together and access elements efficiently. This concept connects to the basic operation (L4) as it provides the context for why arr[i] works. Common beginner mistakes include thinking indices start at 1, confusing array length with last index, or not understanding that arrays have fixed sizes. The real code pattern involves declaring an array like arr = [1,2,3] and understanding its structure.

examples.trace: This array has 3 elements. Position 0 (first) contains 10. Position 1 (second) contains 20. Position 2 (third) contains 30. Length is 3, so valid indices are 0,1,2.
This array has 4 elements. Position 0 contains 5. Position 1 contains 8. Position 2 contains 2. Position 3 contains 9. Length is 4, so valid indices are 0,1,2,3.

approaches.name: Binary Search O(log n)
approaches.summary: Sort the array first, then use binary search to find the insertion point of X, which gives the count of elements smaller than X. This leverages the sorted order for efficient searching.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(log n) for search after O(n log n) sorting
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(1) or O(n) if sorting modifies the array

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Elements Smaller Than X Using Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete solution: sort array, then use binary search to find insertion point of X, giving count of smaller elements.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting elements smaller than a given value X in an array. The binary search approach first sorts the array in O(n log n) time, enabling efficient O(log n) search. The core insight is that in a sorted array, the index where X would be inserted (its insertion point) equals the number of elements strictly smaller than X. For example, if X's insertion point is 3, there are exactly 3 elements smaller than X. The algorithm: 1) Sort the array in non-decreasing order. 2) Perform binary search to find the leftmost position where X could be inserted while maintaining sorted order. This is done by maintaining a search range [left, right] and narrowing it: if arr[mid] < X, move left to mid+1 (X belongs to the right half); otherwise, move right to mid-1 (X belongs at or before mid). After the loop, 'left' holds the insertion point. 3) Return 'left' as the count. Time complexity is O(n log n) for sorting plus O(log n) for binary search, dominated by O(n log n). Space complexity is O(1) if sorting in-place, or O(n) if using extra space. Common mistakes include not handling duplicates correctly (we want strict smaller, so use < comparison), off-by-one errors in binary search bounds, and forgetting to sort first. This approach is efficient for multiple queries on the same array, as sorting is done once.

examples.trace: Step 1: Sort array: [1, 2, 3, 5, 8]. Step 2: Binary search for insertion point of X=4. Initialize left=0, right=4. Iteration 1: mid=(0+4)//2=2, arr[2]=3 < 4, so left=mid+1=3. Iteration 2: left=3, right=4, mid=(3+4)//2=3, arr[3]=5 >= 4, so right=mid-1=2. Loop ends as left=3 > right=2. Insertion point = left = 3. Step 3: Count = 3. Elements smaller than 4: 1,2,3.

Step 1: Array already sorted: [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]. Step 2: Binary search for X=25. left=0, right=4. Iteration 1: mid=2, arr[2]=30 >= 25, right=1. Iteration 2: left=0, right=1, mid=0, arr[0]=10 < 25, left=1. Iteration 3: left=1, right=1, mid=1, arr[1]=20 < 25, left=2. Loop ends as left=2 > right=1. Insertion point = 2. Step 3: Count = 2. Elements smaller: 10,20.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search for Insertion Point
ladders.desc: Find the leftmost index where X can be inserted in a sorted array while maintaining order.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the full problem: given a sorted array and a value X, find the insertion point—the smallest index where X can be placed so the array remains sorted. This index equals the count of elements strictly smaller than X. Intuition: In a sorted array, all elements before the insertion point are < X, and all at or after are >= X. We use binary search to efficiently locate this point in O(log n) time. Algorithm: Initialize left=0, right=n-1 (where n is array length). While left <= right: compute mid = (left+right)//2. If arr[mid] < X, set left = mid+1 (X belongs to the right half). Else, set right = mid-1 (X belongs at or before mid). After the loop, 'left' is the insertion point. For example, in array [1,3,5,7] with X=4, insertion point is 2 (between 3 and 5). Time: O(log n), space: O(1). This feeds into L0 by providing the count after sorting. Common mistakes: using <= instead of < (which would count elements <= X), or incorrect loop condition causing off-by-one errors. Ensure to handle empty arrays by returning 0.

examples.trace: Sorted array: [2,4,6,8]. left=0, right=3. Iteration 1: mid=1, arr[1]=4 < 5, left=2. Iteration 2: left=2, right=3, mid=2, arr[2]=6 >= 5, right=1. Loop ends as left=2 > right=1. Insertion point = 2.
Array sorted: [10,20,30]. left=0, right=2. Iteration 1: mid=1, arr[1]=20 < 30, left=2. Iteration 2: left=2, right=2, mid=2, arr[2]=30 >= 30, right=1. Loop ends: left=2 > right=1. Insertion point = 2.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Insertion Point Equals Count of Smaller Elements
ladders.desc: The insight that in a sorted array, the insertion index of X corresponds to the number of elements < X.
ladders.explanation: This is the central technique: understanding why the insertion point from binary search gives the count of elements smaller than X. Intuition: When an array is sorted in non-decreasing order, all elements before a given index are less than or equal to those at or after that index. Specifically, if we find the leftmost position where X can be inserted while keeping the array sorted, then all elements to the left of this position must be strictly less than X (because if any were >= X, X would need to be inserted earlier). Conversely, elements at or to the right are >= X. Thus, the insertion index directly equals the count of elements < X. For example, in sorted array [1,3,5,7], inserting X=4 at index 2 means there are 2 elements (1 and 3) smaller than 4. This logic relies on strict inequality (<) to count only smaller elements. It connects to L1 by explaining why the binary search subroutine works. Common mistakes: confusing with count of elements <= X (which would use <= comparison), or not considering duplicates (e.g., in [1,1,2], insertion point of 1 is 0, correctly counting 0 smaller elements). This insight is key to efficient O(log n) counting after sorting.

examples.trace: Sorted array: [2,5,8,12]. Insertion point for 9 is 3 (between 8 and 12). Elements before index 3: [2,5,8] all < 9. Thus, count = 3.
Sorted array: [1,1,3,3]. Insertion point for 3 is 2 (first occurrence of 3). Elements before index 2: [1,1] all < 3. Thus, count = 2.

ladders.title: Building Block — Sorting an Array
ladders.desc: Arrange array elements in non-decreasing order as a prerequisite for binary search.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept is sorting: rearranging the elements of an array so they are in ascending order (e.g., from smallest to largest). It's needed because binary search requires a sorted input to work correctly. Intuition: Sorting organizes data so that comparisons can efficiently narrow down search ranges. For this problem, we sort to enable the insertion point logic. Common sorting algorithms include quicksort, mergesort, or built-in sorts like Python's sorted() or list.sort(). Algorithm (using built-in sort): simply call sort() on the array. For example, [5,2,8,1,3] becomes [1,2,3,5,8]. Time complexity: O(n log n) for efficient sorts. Space: O(1) for in-place sorts, or O(n) if using extra memory. This connects to L2 by providing the sorted array needed to apply the insertion point insight. Common mistakes: not sorting before binary search (which would give incorrect results), or using inefficient O(n^2) sorts for large arrays. Ensure to handle empty or single-element arrays trivially.

examples.trace: Original array: [4,1,3,2]. Apply sorting algorithm (e.g., quicksort steps): choose pivot 4, partition: [1,3,2] and [], recurse on left: sort [1,3,2] -> pivot 1, partition: [] and [3,2], sort [3,2] -> [2,3], combine: [1,2,3]. Combine with pivot 4: [1,2,3,4].
Original: [10,5,20]. Sort using built-in: compare and swap. Step 1: compare 10 and 5, swap -> [5,10,20]. Step 2: compare 10 and 20, no swap. Sorted: [5,10,20].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Compare and Swap in Sorting
ladders.desc: Fundamental operation: compare two elements and swap them if out of order.
ladders.explanation: This is a fundamental operation used in many sorting algorithms: comparing two elements and swapping their positions if they are not in the desired order (e.g., if the first is greater than the second for ascending sort). Intuition: By repeatedly comparing and swapping adjacent or selected elements, we can gradually move elements to their correct sorted positions. For example, in bubble sort, we compare adjacent pairs and swap if needed; in insertion sort, we compare each element with previous ones. Algorithm: given two indices i and j in an array arr, if arr[i] > arr[j] for ascending sort, swap them using a temporary variable: temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[j]; arr[j] = temp. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). This connects to L3 as a building block for sorting algorithms. Common mistakes: forgetting to use a temp variable (direct assignment can lose data), or incorrect comparison operator (e.g., using < instead of >). Practice with simple arrays to master this operation.

examples.trace: Initial: [5,3]. Compare arr[0]=5 and arr[1]=3. Since 5 > 3, swap. Step: temp=5, arr[0]=3, arr[1]=5. Result: [3,5].
Initial: [2,4,1]. Compare arr[1]=4 and arr[2]=1. Since 4 > 1, swap. Step: temp=4, arr[1]=1, arr[2]=4. Result: [2,1,4].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Element Access
ladders.desc: The simplest prerequisite: accessing elements in an array using indices.
ladders.explanation: This is the foundational concept for beginners: an array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory locations, each accessible by an index (usually starting at 0). Intuition: Think of an array like a row of boxes numbered 0,1,2,...; each box holds a value, and you can look inside or change the value by referring to its number. For example, in array arr = [10,20,30], arr[0] gives 10, arr[1] gives 20. This is essential for all higher levels because sorting, comparing, and binary search all rely on accessing elements via indices. Operation: to access element at index i, use arr[i]. To modify, assign: arr[i] = newValue. Time: O(1) constant time access. Space: O(n) for storing n elements. This connects to L4 as the basic way to get elements for comparison. Common mistakes: using indices out of bounds (e.g., arr[3] for length 3 array, which would error), or confusing 0-based indexing with 1-based. Always ensure index is within 0 to length-1.

examples.trace: Array: [5,8,2]. Indices: 0->5, 1->8, 2->2. Access arr[1]: go to index 1, value is 8.
Initial: [100,200]. Modify arr[0]: set arr[0] = 150. Now array becomes [150,200].

approaches.name: Frequency-Based O(n + k)
approaches.summary: Use a frequency array or hashmap to count occurrences of each element, then sum frequencies of elements less than X. This is efficient for repeated queries on the same array.
approaches.timeComplexity: O(n + k) where k is the range of values

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Elements Smaller Than X Using Frequency Array
ladders.desc: Complete solution using frequency array for repeated queries on static arrays
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting elements smaller than a given value X in an array. The frequency-based approach is optimal when we have multiple queries on the same static array. The intuition is to preprocess the array once to build a frequency distribution, then answer queries in O(k) time where k is the range of values. This works because we can sum frequencies of all values less than X to get the count. First, we find the minimum and maximum values in the array to determine the range. We create a frequency array of size (max-min+1) initialized to zeros. We iterate through the array, incrementing frequency[arr[i]-min] for each element. For a query X, we calculate the sum of frequencies from index 0 to (X-min-1) if X > min, otherwise return 0. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) Find min and max values in array. 2) Create frequency array of size (max-min+1). 3) Populate frequency array by counting occurrences. 4) For query X, if X <= min, return 0. 5) Otherwise, sum frequencies from index 0 to (X-min-1). Time complexity is O(n + k) for preprocessing and O(k) per query, where k is the range size. Space complexity is O(k) for the frequency array. This connects to the key subroutine of building the frequency array. Common mistakes include: not handling X values outside the array range correctly, off-by-one errors in index calculation, and forgetting to handle empty arrays. Real code pattern: def count_smaller(arr, X): min_val, max_val = min(arr), max(arr); freq = [0]*(max_val-min_val+1); for num in arr: freq[num-min_val] += 1; if X <= min_val: return 0; return sum(freq[:X-min_val])

examples.trace: Step 1: Find min=1, max=9. Step 2: Create freq array size=9 (indices 0-8). Step 3: Populate: arr[0]=3 → freq[3-1]=freq[2]++=1; arr[1]=1 → freq[0]++=1; arr[2]=4 → freq[3]++=1; arr[3]=1 → freq[0]++=2; arr[4]=5 → freq[4]++=1; arr[5]=9 → freq[8]++=1. Final freq=[2,0,1,1,1,0,0,0,1]. Step 4: X=4, min=1 → X-min=3. Step 5: Sum freq[0] to freq[2] = 2+0+1 = 3. Wait, correction: X=4 means elements <4 are 1,1,3 → count should be 4. Error in trace: X-min=3, we need indices <3, so sum freq[0]+freq[1]+freq[2]=2+0+1=3, but we're missing one. Actually, elements are 1,1,3,4,5,9. Elements <4: 1,1,3 → count=3. My initial output was wrong. Let me recalculate: arr sorted: [1,1,3,4,5,9]. Elements <4: 1,1,3 → count=3. So output should be 3. Trace correction: Step 5: X=4, X-min=3, sum freq[0] to freq[2] = freq[0]=2 + freq[1]=0 + freq[2]=1 = 3. Output: 3.

Step 1: min=10, max=50. Step 2: freq array size=41 (indices 0-40). Step 3: Populate: arr[0]=10 → freq[0]++=1; arr[1]=20 → freq[10]++=1; arr[2]=30 → freq[20]++=1; arr[3]=40 → freq[30]++=1; arr[4]=50 → freq[40]++=1. Step 4: X=35, min=10 → X-min=25. Step 5: Sum freq[0] to freq[24] = only indices with values: freq[0]=1 (value 10), freq[10]=1 (value 20), freq[20]=1 (value 30) = total 3. Elements <35: 10,20,30 → count=3.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Building and Querying Frequency Array
ladders.desc: Create frequency array from array and answer count queries using prefix sums
ladders.explanation: The key subroutine in the frequency-based approach is constructing a frequency array from an input array and using it to answer 'count elements less than X' queries efficiently. The intuition is that by storing counts of each value in a frequency array, we can quickly determine how many elements are below a threshold by summing relevant frequencies. This works because the frequency array provides direct access to counts for each possible value. The algorithm: 1) Determine the range of values (min and max). 2) Initialize a frequency array of size (max-min+1) with zeros. 3) Iterate through the input array, for each element increment the corresponding index in frequency array (index = value - min). 4) For a query X, if X <= min, return 0. 5) Otherwise, calculate the sum of frequencies from index 0 to (X-min-1). Time complexity is O(n) for building plus O(k) per query where k is range size. Space complexity is O(k). This connects to the full problem as the core implementation component. Common mistakes: incorrect index calculation (off-by-one), not handling X outside range properly, and inefficient summing for each query. Real code pattern: def build_and_query(arr, X): min_val, max_val = min(arr), max(arr); freq = [0]*(max_val-min_val+1); for num in arr: freq[num-min_val] += 1; if X <= min_val: return 0; return sum(freq[:X-min_val])

examples.trace: Step 1: min=2, max=4. Step 2: freq size=3 (indices: 0→value2, 1→value3, 2→value4). Step 3: Populate: arr[0]=2 → freq[0]++=1; arr[1]=3 → freq[1]++=1; arr[2]=2 → freq[0]++=2; arr[3]=4 → freq[2]++=1. Final freq=[2,1,1]. Step 4: X=3, min=2 → X-min=1. Step 5: Sum freq[0] to freq[0] (since X-min-1=0) = freq[0]=2. Elements <3: two 2's → count=2.
Step 1: min=5, max=5. Step 2: freq size=1 (index 0→value5). Step 3: Populate: all elements 5 → freq[0]=3. Step 4: X=5, min=5 → X <= min, so return 0. Elements <5: none → count=0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Frequency Accumulation for Range Queries
ladders.desc: Sum frequencies of values in a specific range to answer counting queries
ladders.explanation: The core logic of the frequency-based approach is accumulating frequencies of values within a specified range to determine how many elements satisfy a condition. The intuition is that if we have counts of each distinct value, we can answer 'how many elements are less than X' by summing counts of all values strictly less than X. This works because each element's value determines which frequency bucket it belongs to, and summing relevant buckets gives the total count. The algorithm: Given a frequency array freq where freq[i] represents count of value (i + min_value), and a query value X, we need to compute sum(freq[i]) for all i where (i + min_value) < X. This translates to summing freq[0] to freq[X-min_value-1] if X > min_value, else 0. The key insight is converting the value comparison to index range calculation. Time complexity is O(r) where r is the number of indices to sum (at most range size). Space complexity is O(1) additional space beyond the frequency array. This connects to the key subroutine as the query processing step. Common mistakes: incorrect boundary handling when X equals min_value, off-by-one errors in index calculation, and not optimizing the sum operation. Real code pattern: def count_using_freq(freq, min_val, X): if X <= min_val: return 0; idx = X - min_val; return sum(freq[:idx])

examples.trace: Step 1: freq indices: 0→value10, 1→value11, 2→value12, 3→value13. Step 2: X=13, min_val=10 → X-min_val=3. Step 3: Need values <13 → values 10,11,12 → indices 0,1,2. Step 4: Sum freq[0]+freq[1]+freq[2] = 2+1+0 = 3. Explanation: freq[0]=2 means two 10s, freq[1]=1 means one 11, freq[2]=0 means zero 12s, total 3 elements <13.
Step 1: freq indices: 0→value5, 1→value6, 2→value7. Step 2: X=5, min_val=5 → X <= min_val, so return 0. No values less than 5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Creating Frequency Array from Input Array
ladders.desc: Transform an array into a frequency count array indexed by value
ladders.explanation: This building block involves converting an input array into a frequency array that counts occurrences of each distinct value. The intuition is that by creating a direct mapping from values to their counts, we enable efficient query operations later. This works because array elements are discrete values that can be used as indices (after offset adjustment) in a new array. The algorithm: 1) Find the minimum and maximum values in the input array to determine the range. 2) Create an array 'freq' of size (max-min+1) initialized with zeros. 3) Iterate through each element in the input array. 4) For each element value v, calculate index = v - min. 5) Increment freq[index] by 1. Time complexity is O(n) where n is array length. Space complexity is O(k) where k is the range size (max-min+1). This connects to the core logic as it provides the frequency array needed for accumulation. Common mistakes: not calculating min/max correctly for empty arrays, creating unnecessarily large frequency arrays when range is wide, and integer overflow for large values. Real code pattern: def create_freq_array(arr): if not arr: return [], 0; min_val = min(arr); max_val = max(arr); freq = [0]*(max_val-min_val+1); for num in arr: freq[num-min_val] += 1; return freq, min_val

examples.trace: Step 1: Find min=2, max=5. Step 2: Range size = 5-2+1=4? Wait, values: 2,3,4,5 → size=4. But arr has values 2,3,5 only. Let me recalculate: min=2, max=5 → max-min+1=4. Step 3: Create freq array of size 4: indices 0→value2, 1→value3, 2→value4, 3→value5. Step 4: Iterate: arr[0]=5 → index=5-2=3 → freq[3]++=1; arr[1]=2 → index=0 → freq[0]++=1; arr[2]=5 → index=3 → freq[3]++=2; arr[3]=3 → index=1 → freq[1]++=1; arr[4]=2 → index=0 → freq[0]++=2. Final freq=[2,1,0,2], min_val=2. Output: freq=[2,1,0,2], min_val=2.
Step 1: min=10, max=10. Step 2: Range size=1. Step 3: Create freq=[0]. Step 4: Iterate: all elements 10 → index=10-10=0 → freq[0] becomes 3. Final freq=[3], min_val=10.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Counting Occurrences with Array Indexing
ladders.desc: Use array indices to count how many times specific values appear
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using an array's indices to count occurrences of values. The intuition is that if we have a fixed range of possible values, we can use each value as an index (with possible offset) in a counting array to incrementally track frequencies. This works because array indexing provides O(1) access, allowing us to update counts efficiently as we process elements. The algorithm: Given an array 'counts' initialized to zeros and a value v within the range [0, len(counts)-1] (or with offset), we increment counts[v] (or counts[v-offset]) by 1. This is repeated for each element in the input data. Time complexity is O(1) per increment. Space complexity is O(m) for the counts array where m is the range size. This connects to the building block as the fundamental step in creating a frequency array. Common mistakes: accessing indices outside array bounds, not initializing the counts array to zeros, and misunderstanding the offset calculation. Real code pattern: def count_occurrences(values, offset=0): max_val = max(values) if values else -1; counts = [0]*(max_val-offset+1); for v in values: counts[v-offset] += 1; return counts

examples.trace: Step 1: Determine size: max value=4, offset=2 → max-offset=2, size=3 (indices 0,1,2). Step 2: Initialize counts=[0,0,0]. Step 3: Process values[0]=2 → index=2-2=0 → counts[0]++=1. Step 4: values[1]=3 → index=3-2=1 → counts[1]++=1. Step 5: values[2]=2 → index=0 → counts[0]++=2. Step 6: values[3]=4 → index=4-2=2 → counts[2]++=1. Final counts=[2,1,1].

Step 1: max=1, offset=0 → size=2. Step 2: counts=[0,0]. Step 3: values[0]=0 → index=0 → counts[0]++=1. Step 4: values[1]=1 → index=1 → counts[1]++=1. Step 5: values[2]=0 → index=0 → counts[0]++=2. Final counts=[2,1].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Array Indexing and Value-Index Mapping
ladders.desc: Understand how to use array indices to represent and access data values
ladders.explanation: This foundational concept covers using array indices to store and retrieve information based on values. The intuition is that in programming, arrays are contiguous memory locations accessed by integer indices starting from 0. We can use these indices to represent other integer values by establishing a mapping relationship. For example, if we want to store data for values 5, 6, and 7, we can use array indices 0, 1, 2 and map value v to index (v-5). This works because subtraction creates a consistent offset that aligns values with indices. The algorithm: 1) Determine the range of values you want to represent. 2) Calculate the minimum value (min_val). 3) Create an array of size (max_val-min_val+1). 4) To access or modify data for a value v, use index = v - min_val. Time complexity for access is O(1). Space complexity is O(range size). This connects to the basic operation as it enables counting occurrences by value. Common mistakes: forgetting that array indices must be non-negative integers, miscalculating the array size, and not handling values outside the expected range. Real code pattern: def create_mapped_array(min_val, max_val): size = max_val - min_val + 1; arr = [0]*size; def set_value(v, data): arr[v-min_val] = data; def get_value(v): return arr[v-min_val]; return arr, set_value, get_value

examples.trace: Step 1: Calculate size = 12-10+1=3. Step 2: Create arr = [0,0,0] of size 3. Step 3: Map value 10 to index 10-10=0 → set arr[0]='A'. Step 4: Value 11 to index 11-10=1 → arr[1]='B'. Step 5: Value 12 to index 12-10=2 → arr[2]='C'. Final arr=['A','B','C'].
Step 1: size = 5-5+1=1. Step 2: arr = [0]. Step 3: Value 5 to index 5-5=0 → arr[0]='X'. Final arr=['X'].
